1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim$. The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve $C$ over
39	$k$. As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	$\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	$k$-scheme $T$ and
42	$\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)$ for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~\ref{sec:relpic_group}
52	and \S~\ref{sec:relpic_groupvalued}) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab$ on the category of $k$-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~\ref{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim$ in the \'etale topology on
58	$(\Sch/k)^{op}$, and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group $\Pic(X)$ of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of $[\mathcal{L}]$
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	$\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)$ of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules sends $\mathcal{O}_T$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
75	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
76	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
77	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
78	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
92	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on $\Pic$).}
97	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let $T$ be a
98	  $k$-scheme. The Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$, and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly $H_T$.
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
124	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
125	  The abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ under tensor product is
126	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
127	  $\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'$, the tensor product
128	  $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'$ is again
129	  invertible, the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$ is the unit, and
130	  the dual $\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})$ is the
131	  inverse. Pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules sends
132	  $\mathcal{O}_T$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$ (the structure sheaf is
133	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
134	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
135	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
136	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore $\pi_T^*$
137	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
138	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
139	
140	  The image $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is then a subgroup. The quotient set
141	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ of
142	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
143	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
144	  abelian quotient): for $\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)$, set
145	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']$;
146	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
147	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ is by construction a
148	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly $H_T$.
149	\end{proof}

152	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}

155	group-valued presheaf.

191	\begin{lemma}[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, lem:pullback_compose, lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid,
195	        thm:pullback_natural}
196	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
197	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
198	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
199	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
200	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
201	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
204	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
205	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
206	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
207	  %  [\dots]
208	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
209	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by

211	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
212	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
213	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
214	  For a morphism $g : T' \to T$ of $k$-schemes, the induced set map
215	  $g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')$ of
216	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
217	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
218	  composition laws $\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ and
219	  $(g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp$ hold as identities of group

222	
223	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
224	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
225	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument

241	  composition laws are inherited from $\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}$ and

243	\end{proof}

288	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
289	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
290	
291	The presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
292	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a $k$-scheme
293	$T$ carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to $C \times_k T$ becomes
294	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
295	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
296	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
297	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
298	this sheaf $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
299	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
104	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
105	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
106	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
107	Picard functor at `T`.
108	
109	iter-175+: instantiate as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrying an
110	invertibility witness — concretely either
111	```
112	structure OnProduct ... where
113	  sheaf : (pullback πC πT).Modules
114	  isInvertible : IsInvertible sheaf
115	```
116	or the quotient by isomorphism for the Picard *group* refinement. For the
117	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` at the type level because
118	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`. -/
119	noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (_πC : C ⟶ S) (_πT : T ⟶ S) :
120	    Type (u+1) :=
121	  sorry

123	/-! ## §2. The pullback functor along the projection
124	
125	For a morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ Z` and an invertible `O_Z`-module `M`, the
126	pullback `f^* M` is an invertible `O_Y`-module (the pullback of an
127	open-cover-trivialisation of `M` trivialises `f^* M`, and the pullback of a free
128	module of rank one is free of rank one — Stacks tag 01HH). Specialising to the
129	projection `π_T = pullback.snd πC πT : C ×_S T ⟶ T` gives the pullback map
130	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`. Mathlib's underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback`
131	functor (in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) supplies the pullback on
132	the full category of sheaves of modules; iter-175+ will restrict it to
133	invertible sheaves once the `OnProduct` carrier is unpacked.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:pullback_along_projection` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag
136	01HH). -/
137	
138	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
139	curve. Sends an `O_T`-line bundle `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-line bundle
140	`π_T^* N`, induced by the underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
141	`Scheme.Modules`.
142	
143	iter-175+: instantiate as `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`
144	wrapped with the carried `IsInvertible` witness (the pullback of an invertible
145	sheaf is invertible, Stacks 01HH). For the iter-174 file-skeleton the body is
146	a typed `sorry` because the carrier type `OnProduct` is itself a typed `sorry`
147	(see §1). The signature is non-tautological: it asserts a *function*
148	`T.Modules → OnProduct πC πT`, which after unfolding `OnProduct` becomes the
149	concrete pullback functor restricted to invertible modules. -/
150	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
151	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (_N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=
152	  sorry

154	/-! ## §3. Composition of pullbacks
155	
156	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
157	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
158	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
159	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.
160	
161	Specialised to the relative curve setting: given `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the
162	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
163	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
164	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the
165	canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors via the pseudo-functoriality
166	of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` (Mathlib `Modules.pullbackComp`).
167	
168	Blueprint reference: `lem:pullback_compose` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
169	
170	/-- **Composition of line-bundle pullbacks** along the projection. For a base
171	scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects `T, T'` over `S`
172	via `πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
173	(encoded by the hypothesis `πT' = g ≫ πT`), set
174	`g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T`
175	(the base-change morphism, given by `pullback.map`). On the underlying
176	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` functors the two routes through the canonical
177	naturality square give canonically isomorphic objects:
178	```
179	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)
180	  T.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T).Modules
181	       │                                                              │
182	       │ Scheme.Modules.pullback g            Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C
183	       ▼                                                              ▼
184	 T'.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T').Modules
185	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')
186	```
187	i.e. for any `N : T.Modules`,
188	```
189	(Modules.pullback g_C).obj ((Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N)
190	  ≅
191	(Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((Modules.pullback g).obj N).
192	```
193	
194	This natural isomorphism is the substantive content of the
195	"line-bundle pullback functor is well-behaved under composition" fact
196	(Stacks tag 01HG). At the level of the `OnProduct` carrier (which is a typed
197	`sorry` in iter-174; see §1) it descends to equality of isomorphism classes
198	`[g_C^* π_T^* N] = [π_{T'}^* g^* N] ∈ OnProduct πC πT'`.
199	
200	iter-175+: the body is `Modules.pullbackComp` composed twice (once on each
201	route) and patched together by the equation `πT' = g ≫ πT` (which gives
202	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` from the pullback square). For the iter-174
203	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
204	theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
205	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
206	    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
207	    Nonempty
208	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
209	            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
210	              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
211	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
212	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
213	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
214	  sorry
215	
216	end LineBundle

220	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
221	
222	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
223	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
224	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
225	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
226	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
227	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
228	Picard presheaf at `T`.
229	
230	The "preimage_subgroup" name follows the Kleiman/Stacks convention: the
231	quotient is by the preimage `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)`, treated as a
232	subgroup (image of a group homomorphism); the project-side Lean encoding
233	extracts the *Setoid* that the quotient construction takes.
234	
235	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined` (Kleiman §2,
236	Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
237	
238	/-- **Well-definedness of the relative Picard quotient as a `Setoid`.**
239	
240	The equivalence relation on `OnProduct πC πT` defining the relative Picard
241	presheaf
242	```
243	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
244	```
245	is the relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`. This relation is reflexive
246	(`L ⊗ L⁻¹ = O_(C ×_S T) = π_T^* O_T ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`), symmetric (the inverse of
247	a line bundle in `π_T^* Pic(T)` is in `π_T^* Pic(T)`), and transitive
248	(`L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` and `L' ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` give
249	`L ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`); hence is an equivalence relation, encoded by a
250	`Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT`.
251	
252	The substantive content is the well-definedness of the quotient: the canonical
253	projection `OnProduct πC πT → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the
254	set-theoretic underlying map of the relative Picard presheaf at `T`.
255	
256	iter-175+: the body constructs the `Setoid` from the explicit relation `L ~ L'
257	↔ ∃ N : T.Modules, L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ≅ pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N`. For the
258	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` because `OnProduct` itself is a
259	typed `sorry` (so the tensor product `L ⊗ L'⁻¹` on the carrier is not yet
260	typed). -/
261	noncomputable def preimage_subgroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
262	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
263	    Setoid (LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) :=
264	  sorry
265	
266	/-! ## §5. Naturality in the test scheme — the relative Picard presheaf as a functor
267	
268	For a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S` (encoded by the equality `πT' = g ≫ πT`),
269	the base-change `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` induces a map
270	`g_C^* : Pic(C ×_S T) → Pic(C ×_S T')` on line bundles, which (by
271	`lem:pullback_compose` = `pullback_pullback_eq` above) sends the subgroup
272	`π_T^* Pic(T)` into `π_{T'}^* Pic(T')`. Therefore it descends to a unique map
273	on quotients
274	```
275	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T'),    [L] ↦ [g_C^* L],
276	```
277	making `Pic^♯_{C/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` a presheaf.
278	
279	For the iter-174 file-skeleton, we pin the **object-level** functorial action
280	(i.e. the induced map of quotient sets for a single test morphism `g`). The
281	full `Functor` packaging (with the identity / composition laws supplied as
282	`map_id`, `map_comp` fields) is iter-175+ work once `OnProduct` and the
283	`preimage_subgroup` carrier are unpacked.
284	
285	Blueprint reference: `thm:pullback_natural` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
286	functor" + Def. `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01HG). -/

289	
290	Object-level functorial action: for any morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, there

292	```
293	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')
294	```
295	of quotient sets, factoring `g_C^*` through `preimage_subgroup πC πT` and

297	`g_C := id_C ×_S g`. The factorisation through the quotient is well-defined
298	because `g_C^*` sends `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)` into
299	`π_{T'}^* Pic(T') ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T')` (this is the commutative-square content of

308	map between two distinct quotient sets that depends on the morphism `g`. -/
309	noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
310	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T) (_hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :

137	/-! ## §1. Coherent-sheaf flatness over a base
138	
139	For a morphism `f : X ⟶ S` of schemes and a coherent (more generally:
140	quasi-coherent) `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, the *coherent-sheaf flatness* predicate
141	`CoherentSheafFlat f 𝓕` captures Stacks 00HB lifted to the sheaf level:
142	for every point `x ∈ X` the stalk `𝓕_x` is a flat `𝓞_{S, f(x)}`-module
143	via the composite `𝓞_{S, f(x)} → 𝓞_{X, x} → End(𝓕_x)`.
144	
145	Affine-locally (Stacks 00HB equivalence): on an affine open `U = Spec A
146	⊆ S` and an affine open `V = Spec B ⊆ f⁻¹U` with `𝓕|_V = M̃` for a
147	finite `B`-module `M`, the condition becomes `Module.Flat A M` with the
148	`A`-module structure inherited via the ring map `A → B`. The
149	file-internal encoding uses this affine-local form.
150	
151	Blueprint reference: `def:coherent_sheaf_flat`. -/
152	
153	/-- A coherent sheaf `𝓕` on `X` is **flat over `S`** for a morphism
154	`f : X ⟶ S` if, for every affine open `U ⊆ S` and every affine open
155	`V ⊆ f⁻¹U`, the `𝓞_X`-module of sections `Γ(𝓕, V)` is flat as a
156	`Γ(S, U)`-module, where the `Γ(S, U)`-action is the restriction of
157	scalars along the ring morphism `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V)` (the `appLE`
158	morphism of `f`).
159	
160	This is the affine-local form of Stacks 00HB (a coherent sheaf is flat
161	over the base if every stalk is flat over the corresponding base
162	stalk); the affine-local form and stalk form are equivalent for
163	*quasi-coherent* sheaves on a noetherian base, which is the regime of
164	the flattening-stratification theorem.
165	
166	iter-177+: the body of subsequent flat-locus theorems may package this
167	predicate as an instance-driven typeclass `Module.Flat`-style if the
168	sheafified-tensor infrastructure lands in Mathlib; for the iter-176
169	file-skeleton it is encoded as a `Prop` quantifier. -/
170	def CoherentSheafFlat {X S : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ S) (F : X.Modules) : Prop :=
171	  ∀ {U : S.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen U) {V : X.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen V)
172	    (e : V ≤ f ⁻¹ᵁ U),
173	    letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V) := Module.compHom _ (f.appLE U V e).hom
174	    Module.Flat Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V)
175	
176	end Scheme

178	/-! ## §2. Generic flatness (Nitsure §4)
179	
180	Over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type
181	`X ⟶ S` is flat above some non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. This is the inductive
182	engine of the flattening-stratification theorem: combined with
183	Noetherian induction on the closed complement `S ∖ V`, it produces the
184	finite stratification of `S` by flatness loci.
185	
186	Algebraically (theorem `generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin): for a
187	noetherian domain `A`, a finite-type `A`-algebra `B`, and a finite
188	`B`-module `M`, there exists a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that `M_f` is a
189	free `A_f`-module. The geometric form (this declaration) restricts to a
190	non-empty affine open `Spec A ⊆ S` and applies the algebraic form on
191	each finite-type-algebra patch of `X` above `Spec A`.
192	
193	Blueprint reference: `thm:generic_flatness` (Nitsure §4). -/
194	
195	/-- **Generic flatness theorem** (Nitsure §4 / Stacks ?).
196	
197	For a noetherian integral scheme `S`, a finite-type morphism `p : X ⟶ S`,
198	and a coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a non-empty open subscheme
199	`V ⊆ S` such that `𝓕|_{X_V} = 𝓕|_{p⁻¹V}` is flat over `𝓞_V`.

322	/-! ## §4. Existence of the flattening stratification (Nitsure §4 main)
323	
324	The main theorem combines the three sub-lemmas above: starting from a
325	proper morphism `π : X ⟶ S` and a coherent sheaf `𝓕`, one obtains a
326	finite locally-closed stratification of `S` indexed by Hilbert
327	polynomials, on each piece of which `𝓕` becomes flat, with all four
328	properties of Nitsure's Theorem packaged in a single conjunction.

330	Blueprint reference: `thm:flattening_stratification_exists`
331	(Nitsure §4; Stacks 052H). -/
332	
333	/-- **Flattening stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4 main /
334	Stacks 052H].
335	
336	For a noetherian scheme `S`, a proper morphism `π : X ⟶ S`, and a
337	coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a finite locally-closed
338	stratification `{S_f}` of `S` indexed by a finite set `I` such that
339	- each `ι : S_f ⟶ S` is a (locally-closed) immersion;
340	- the underlying sets `|S_f|` partition `|S|` (disjoint and covering);
341	- the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` is flat over `S_f` for each `f`.
342	
343	The (intended) substantive refinement is that the index set `I` is in
344	bijection with the set of Hilbert polynomials arising on fibres, and
345	that each `S_f` is uniquely determined by its Hilbert polynomial. For
346	the iter-176 file-skeleton the substantive type captures the
347	stratification + flatness; the Hilbert-polynomial labeling is encoded
348	in `flatLocusAssembly`'s `P : I → ℕ → ℤ` injection but elided here for
349	type-clarity.
350	
351	iter-177+: the body assembles `flatLocusReduction` (Lemma 6) ⟶
352	`flatLocusAssembly` (Lemma 7). The structural skeleton is encoded
353	directly here; the Mathlib-side ingredients required (relative
354	projective space `ℙⁿ_S`, Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity, direct image
355	base-change `R^r π_* 𝓕(m) = 0` for `m ≫ 0`, cohomology-and-base-change
356	Stacks tag 02KH for the `H^0` form) are itemised in the blueprint
357	chapter §`Mathlib status`. -/
358	theorem flatteningStratification {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]

369	
370	Nitsure's part (ii): the morphism `i : ⨿_f S_f → S` assembled from the
371	stratum inclusions is universal for "`𝓕` becomes `T`-flat after pullback
372	along `T → S`". Equivalently, for any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that
373	`𝓕|_{X ×_S T}` is `T`-flat, `φ` factors uniquely through some `S_f`.
374	
375	For the iter-176 file-skeleton we encode the universal property in the
376	factor-through form (∃! factorisation through one of the strata
377	`ι f : S_f ⟶ S`), which is the substantive Yoneda-bijection content of
378	Nitsure's part (ii).

380	Blueprint reference: `thm:flattening_stratification_universal`
381	(Nitsure §4 (ii); Stacks 052H). -/

387	by `flatteningStratification`), a morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that the

402	    [∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)] :

404	      Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π φ)
405	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π φ)).obj F) →
406	      ∃! (data : (f : I) × (T ⟶ S_ f)),
407	        data.2 ≫ ι data.1 = φ := by

410	/-! ## §6. Curve specialisation (Route A consumer)
411	
412	In the Route A consumer setting, the input morphism is the second
413	projection `pr_T : C ×_k T ⟶ T` of the pullback of a smooth proper
414	curve `C/k` against a noetherian `k`-scheme `T`. A smooth proper curve
415	over a field is automatically projective (Hartshorne II.6.7 / II.7.6;
416	folklore), hence in particular proper; the relative-curve `C ×_k T → T`
417	inherits properness by base change, hence the hypotheses of the main
418	theorem are met. This corollary records the specialisation
419	explicitly. -/

421	/-- **Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative
422	curve** [Nitsure §4 corollary; Route~A consumer A.2.a entry-point].
423	
424	Let `k` be a field, `C` a smooth proper curve over `k` (encoded as
425	`C : Over (Spec k)` with `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]` and

427	sheaf `𝓕` on the relative curve `C ×_k T`, the flattening
428	stratification conclusion of `flatteningStratification` applies to

430	stratification `{T_f ⊆ T}` set-theoretically covering `T` disjointly,
431	such that `𝓕|_{C ×_k T_f}` is flat over `T_f` for each `f`.

433	iter-177+: the body invokes `flatteningStratification` on the
434	base-changed morphism `pullback.snd C.hom T.hom : (C ×_k T) ⟶ T`,
435	using that `IsProper (pullback.snd C.hom T.hom)` holds by base change
436	of `IsProper C.hom`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a
437	typed `sorry`. -/

448	        (pullback.snd (pullback.snd C.hom T.hom) (ι f))

9	# The Quot scheme (A.2.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
14	`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` — a projective `S`-scheme representing the functor of
15	`T`-flat coherent quotients of `E_T` on `X_T = X ×_S T` with Hilbert
16	polynomial `Φ` on every fiber — together with the in-project sub-build for
17	the Grassmannian *scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a
18	linear-algebra Grassmannian).
19

35	   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a

41	   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on

57	   very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent `E`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the functor

61	   **flat base-change theorem of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH): for a
62	   cartesian square with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact quasi-separated,
63	   the canonical base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an

69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
72	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
75	  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
76	  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
77	- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
78	  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
79	- `Grassmannian.representable` and `QuotScheme` package the
80	  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
81	  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive
82	  content (a representable functor is determined by its representing
83	  object up to canonical isomorphism, and the witness is the data of
84	  that isomorphism family).
85	- `flatBaseChangeCohomology` produces a `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` of an
86	  isomorphism between two `S'`-modules built via the pullback/pushforward
87	  bifunctor; the iso is non-trivial (it is `Stacks 02KH` content, not
88	  the identity-on-the-same-object iso `Iso.refl _`).

139	/-! ## §1. Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf
140	
141	For a finite-type morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with `S` noetherian and a coherent
142	sheaf `F` on `X` whose schematic support is proper over `S` (here encoded
143	as plain `X.Modules` for the file-skeleton), the per-fiber Hilbert
144	polynomial is the function
145	
146	`s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ],   Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`.
147	
148	Snapper's Lemma ensures this is a polynomial in `m`; the proof requires
149	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure and is not stated here.
150	
151	Blueprint reference: `def:hilbert_polynomial` (Nitsure §1; cf. Hartshorne
152	III.5.2). -/
153	
154	/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` over `S` at
155	the fiber over `s ∈ S` with respect to a line bundle `L` on `X`.
156	
157	Encoded as a function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]`. The defining formula
158	
159	`Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗ m})
160	            = Σ_i (-1)^i dim_{κ(s)} H^i(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`
161	
162	is a polynomial in `m` by Snapper's Lemma; the polynomial coefficients
163	depend on `s` through the fiber `F|_{X_s}`. When `F` is `S`-flat the
164	function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`.
165	
166	iter-177+: the body unfolds to the graded-Euler-characteristic
167	construction once `χ` of a coherent sheaf on a noetherian scheme +
168	Snapper's polynomial-eventually-property are in scope. For the iter-176
169	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/

172	    Polynomial ℚ :=

175	/-! ## §2. The Quot functor
176	
177	The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
178	set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
179	- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
180	  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
181	- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
182	- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
183	  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
184	
185	Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.
186	
187	The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
188	`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
191	
192	/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`

199	`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo

277	/-! ## §4. Representability of the Quot scheme
278	
279	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman: for a noetherian `S`, a projective
280	`π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent
281	`E` on `X`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is
282	representable by a *projective* `S`-scheme.
283	
284	The proof has four steps (Nitsure §5):
285	1. **Boundedness** via Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity (uniform across
286	   fibers of `π` and across coherent quotients of `E_s` with Hilbert
287	   polynomial `Φ`).
288	2. **Grassmannian embedding**
289	   `α : Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} ↪ Grass(W ⊗_{O_S} Sym^r V, Φ(r))`
290	   for `r ≥ m`, sending `⟨F, q⟩ ↦ ⟨(π_T)_* F(r), (π_T)_*(q(r))⟩`.
291	3. **Locally closed in Grassmannian** via the flattening stratification
292	   applied to the universal cokernel on the Grassmannian, producing the
293	   stratum `T_0^Φ`.
294	4. **Closed embedding** by the valuative criterion of properness for
295	   DVRs.
296	
297	The reduction to the universal case `X = ℙ(V)`, `E = π*W` is recorded as
298	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W` in the blueprint chapter.
299	
300	Blueprint reference: `thm:quot_representable` (Nitsure §5; FGA Explained
301	Ch. 5; Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV). -/
302	
303	/-- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (Grothendieck, Altman–Kleiman).
304	
305	Let `S` be a noetherian scheme, `π : X ⟶ S` a projective morphism (here
306	encoded as a proper `LocallyOfFiniteType` morphism; the projectivity
307	upgrades once `IsProjective` lands in Mathlib), `L` a line bundle on `X`
308	(relatively very ample), `E` a coherent `O_X`-module, and
309	`Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`. Then the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `QuotFunctor`
310	is representable by an `S`-scheme.
311	
312	We package the conclusion as the existence of `Q : Over S` together with
313	a `Functor.RepresentableBy Q` witness for `QuotFunctor π L E Φ`; the
314	*projectivity* of `Q ⟶ S` (and the universal quotient
315	`q^univ : π^*_Q E ↠ F^univ` on `X ×_S Q^{Φ,L}`) is implicit in the
316	construction (Plücker-embedded into a projective Grassmannian over `S`)
317	and is iter-177+ refinement work.
318

import Mathlib
import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback

/-!
# The relative Picard functor and its étale sheafification (A.1.c)

This file is the **A.1.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It upgrades the
set-valued relative Picard presheaf
`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` of
`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean` (A.1.b) to its
abelian-group-valued refinement, and then to its étale sheafification
`Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.

## Status (iter-176 Lane G file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane G died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window at
06:14 UTC without ever calling `Write` (the file was never created).
iter-176 re-dispatches the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned
declaration carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
the `\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex`)
with a `sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work, gated on the A.1.a
`RelativeSpec.lean` and A.1.b `LineBundlePullback.lean` bodies landing
first.

The 5 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup` (instance, ~5 LOC) —
   the **abelian-group instance** on the quotient set
   `Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)` built in A.1.b.
   Mathlib's `QuotientAddGroup` machinery on a normal subgroup of an
   abelian group is the backbone; the project-side work certifies that
   `π_T^*` is a group homomorphism (Stacks 01CR + pullback's
   tensor-product preservation).

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp` (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) —
   the **relative Picard presheaf** as a contravariant functor on
   `(Over (Spec k))^op` with values in `AddCommGrpCat`, sending
   `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` with the canonical abelian-group
   structure of (1).

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial` (noncomputable def,
   ~10 LOC) — the **group-homomorphism strengthening** of the set-valued
   `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b: for `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k`,
   the induced map on quotient sets is in fact an
   `AddMonoidHom`-homomorphism with respect to the structure of (1).

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf` (noncomputable def,
   ~10 LOC) — the **packaged functor**: bundles the data of (2) and (3)
   into a single `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat` instance.

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme` (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) —
   the **étale sheafification** of `PicSharp`. Encoded as
   `presheafToSheaf` applied with `J : GrothendieckTopology` representing
   the étale topology on `Over (Spec k)` (a parameter at this stage
   because Mathlib at the pinned commit does not ship the étale
   Grothendieck topology on schemes; iter-177+ refinement: bind `J` to
   the canonical étale topology once it lands).

Plus one unpinned auxiliary theorem:

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.unit` (theorem, ~8 LOC) — the
   **sheafification unit**: a canonical morphism of presheaves
   `PicSharp ⟶ (forget _) ∘ (PicScheme ?).val` that exhibits the
   group-presheaf-to-group-sheaf universal property (statement of
   `thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` in the blueprint).

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `addCommGroup` is an `AddCommGroup` instance on a quotient set whose
  underlying type is itself a typed `sorry` in A.1.b (`OnProduct` carrier);
  the substantive content is the existence of the group structure
  (closure under `+`, inverse, neutral element), which is non-trivial
  even at the level of typed-`sorry` carriers — once the carrier is
  unpacked, the group operations are induced by the tensor product on
  invertible sheaves modulo the subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)`.

- `PicSharp` returns a contravariant *functor*, not a constant family;
  the object and morphism actions are independently substantive (the
  morphism action is the set-valued `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of
  A.1.b lifted to a group homomorphism).

- `PicSharp.functorial` returns an `AddMonoidHom`, not a bare set map;
  this is a strict strengthening of the set-valued version in A.1.b.

- `PicSharp.presheaf` returns a `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`,
  which on objects matches `PicSharp` and on morphisms matches
  `PicSharp.functorial` — substantive content is the assembly into a
  single category-theoretic object.

- `PicScheme` returns a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat` for the given topology
  `J`; the sheafification is non-trivial because `PicSharp` is generally
  not even a Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2, L1292–L1302).

## Mathlib status

Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:

- `AddCommGrpCat` (the category of abelian groups, in

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

namespace PicSharp

/-! ## §1. Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient

The Picard group `Pic(X)` of any scheme is canonically an abelian group
under tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of
`[L]` is `[L⁻¹] = [Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)]`). The pullback map
`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_k T)` of
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection` (A.1.b)
respects this structure: it sends `O_T ↦ O_{C ×_k T}` (the structure
sheaf is preserved by inverse image) and is multiplicative on tensor
products (Stacks 01HH for invertibility, Mathlib's
`Scheme.Modules.pullback` for the underlying multiplicativity).

Therefore `π_T^* Pic(T)` is a subgroup of `Pic(C ×_k T)`, and the
quotient `Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` inherits a canonical
abelian-group structure under which the quotient map is a surjective
homomorphism with kernel exactly `π_T^* Pic(T)`.

Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01CR). -/

/-- **Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient.**

For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test
object `πT : T ⟶ S`, the quotient set
```
Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)
  = Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
```
carries a canonical abelian-group structure: addition is the descent of
tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']`, the zero element is the class
`[O_{C ×_S T}]`, and the inverse is `-[L] := [L⁻¹]` (the dual line
bundle). Well-definedness on the quotient uses the subgroup property of
`π_T^* Pic(T)` from `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid`.

iter-177+: the body builds the `AddCommGroup` instance via
Mathlib's `QuotientAddGroup` on the abelian group `Pic(C ×_S T)` (tensor
product) modulo the subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)` (image of a group hom).
For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry` because the
underlying carrier `LineBundle.OnProduct` is itself a typed `sorry` in
A.1.b (so no concrete tensor-product law is yet available); the
signature still asserts the substantive group instance on the named
quotient set. -/
noncomputable instance addCommGroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
    AddCommGroup (Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)) :=
  sorry

end PicSharp

/-! ## §2. The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued functor

We assemble the data of A.1.b (object-level quotient set,
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial` morphism action) and §1 (abelian-group
instance on each quotient) into a single contravariant functor

```
PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
```

sending an `Spec k`-scheme `T` to the abelian group
`Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (with the structure of §1), and a
morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k` to the group homomorphism
`g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/k}(T')` descended from the
line-bundle pullback `g_C^* := (id_C ×_k g)^*`.

Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_sharp` (Kleiman §2, Def. `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- The **relative Picard functor** of a smooth proper geometrically
integral curve `C` over a field `k`, as a contravariant functor

```
PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
```

On objects: `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, the abelian-group
quotient of `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Lean instance
`PicSharp.addCommGroup`).

On morphisms: `g ↦ g^♯`, the group homomorphism descended from
`g_C^* = (id_C ×_k g)^*` via the quotient (the set-level map is
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial`; the group-hom upgrade is
`PicSharp.functorial` below).

Universe: object map values are `AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` because the
underlying carrier `Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` lives in
`Type (u+1)` (since `LineBundle.OnProduct` is defined to land in
`Type (u+1)`).

`AddCommGrpCat.of (Quotient (preimage_subgroup C.hom T.hom))` on
objects and from `PicSharp.functorial` on morphisms. The identity and
composition laws follow from `Scheme.Modules.pullbackId` /
`Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp` descended through the quotient. For the
iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def PicSharp {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom] :
    (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

namespace PicSharp

/-! ## §3. Functoriality (group-homomorphism strengthening)

The naturality lemma `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b produces, for
each morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, a set map
```
g^♯ : Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T) ⟶ Pic(C ×_S T') / π_{T'}^* Pic(T')
```
of quotient sets. Combined with the abelian-group instance of §1 on
both sides, this set map upgrades to an additive-monoid homomorphism
`AddMonoidHom`: indeed `g_C^*` preserves tensor products and the
structure sheaf, so it preserves the abelian-group operations on both
sides; the upgrade is the substantive content of
`lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial`.

Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- **Functoriality of the relative Picard presheaf, group-hom form.**

For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects
`πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
(encoded by `πT' = g ≫ πT`), the set map
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial πC πT πT' g hg` upgrades to an
`AddMonoidHom`-homomorphism with respect to the abelian-group structure
of `PicSharp.addCommGroup` on source and target.

iter-177+: the body builds the `AddMonoidHom` from the set map by
verifying `map_zero` (the image of `[O_{C ×_S T}]` is `[O_{C ×_S T'}]`,
i.e. pullback of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf) and
`map_add` (pullback preserves tensor products: Stacks 01HG /
`Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensor`). For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
    (_hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
    Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT) →+
      Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
  sorry

/-! ## §4. Wrapping as a functor instance

The group-valued presheaf bundling: combine the on-objects assignment
of `PicSharp` (carrying the `addCommGroup` structure) with the
on-morphisms assignment of `functorial` (each a group hom) into a
single functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`. The identity /
composition laws of the functor are inherited from the corresponding
identities on `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, descended through the
quotient.

This wrapper is kept distinct from `PicSharp` to mirror the blueprint
split (`def:rel_pic_sharp` vs.\ `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`) and to
record the explicit re-packaging step from the lemma-by-lemma data
(`addCommGroup`, `PicSharp`, `functorial`) into a single
category-theoretic functor — useful when applying functorial
constructions (Yoneda, sheafification, …) to the *bundled* form.

Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- **The relative Picard presheaf, bundled.**

The relative Picard functor `PicSharp_{C/k}` packaged as a single
contravariant functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`, with object
action `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (group structure
`PicSharp.addCommGroup`) and morphism action `g ↦ g^♯` (group hom from
`PicSharp.functorial`). Identity and composition laws are inherited
from `Scheme.Modules.pullbackId` / `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`
descended through the quotient.

iter-177+: the body either re-exports `PicSharp` (if the
`AddCommGrpCat`-valued functor is built directly in §2) or constructs
the functor from the on-objects/on-morphisms data using the
`CategoryTheory.Functor.mk` builder; the body is a typed `sorry` for
the iter-176 file-skeleton.

Note on naming: this is the canonical "fully assembled" form mentioned
as `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` in the blueprint; it is structurally a
`def` (not a `theorem`) because the conclusion is a `Functor` (data),
not a `Prop`. -/
noncomputable def presheaf {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom] :
    (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

end PicSharp

/-! ## §5. Étale sheafification

The relative Picard presheaf `PicSharp_{C/k}` is generally not even a
Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2 L1292–L1302). To obtain a representable
functor in the sense of `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`
(downstream A.2.c), one replaces `PicSharp_{C/k}` by its **associated
étale sheaf** `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{∼ét}`. Kleiman §4
(Theorem `th:main`) represents precisely this sheafified functor.

For the iter-176 file-skeleton, we encode the sheafification as
`presheafToSheaf J _` applied to the bundled `PicSharp.presheaf` for a
**parameter** Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` — because
Mathlib at the pinned commit does not ship an étale Grothendieck
topology on schemes (it ships only the morphism property
`AlgebraicGeometry.Etale`). iter-177+ refinement: bind `J` to the
canonical étale topology once it lands in Mathlib.

Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification` (Kleiman §2,
`df:Pfs` étale-sheaf clause). -/

/-- The **étale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf**.

Given a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C` over a field
`k`, and a Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` (intended: the
canonical étale topology on `(Sch/k)`), the **étale-sheafified relative
Picard functor**

```
Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{~_ét}
```

is the sheafification of the group-valued presheaf
`PicSharp.presheaf` of `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`. Equivalently, it is
the unique (up to canonical isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the
site `(Over (Spec k), J)` equipped with a presheaf morphism
`PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ (forget _) ∘ -` universal among presheaf morphisms
to abelian-group sheaves.

Encoded as a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` object; iter-177+ implementations
will fix `J` to the étale topology and unfold via
`CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf` (Mathlib's
`Sites.ConcreteSheafification`). The body of the iter-176 file-skeleton
is a typed `sorry`.

In Kleiman's notation with `X = C` and `S = Spec k`, this is
`Pic_{(X/S)ét}` from `df:Pfs`. -/
noncomputable def PicScheme {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom]
    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
    Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

namespace PicScheme

/-! ## §6. Sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure

The étale sheafification of an abelian-group-valued presheaf is itself
abelian-group-valued: the forgetful functor `AddCommGrpCat ⥤ Type`
preserves filtered colimits (these are computed pointwise), and the
plus-construction is built from filtered colimits, so sheafification
commutes with the forgetful functor up to canonical isomorphism. The
substantive content is the **sheafification unit**: a canonical
morphism of presheaves `PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ PicScheme.val` (in
`AddCommGrpCat`) that exhibits the universal property of sheafification.

This statement does NOT have an explicit `\lean{...}` pin in the
blueprint (`thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` is a description
rather than a Lean target); we surface it here so the downstream
representability statement (`chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`) can
cite the unit directly.

Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` (Kleiman
§2, `df:Pfs`; standard `Sheafification.toSheafify` content). -/

/-- **Sheafification unit for the étale Picard sheafification.**

Given the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor
`PicScheme C J : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`, there is a canonical morphism
of (group-valued) presheaves
```
η_C : PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicScheme C J).val
```
in the functor category `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`. This
morphism is the **unit of sheafification**: it is universal among
morphisms from `PicSharp.presheaf C` to any abelian-group-valued
étale sheaf.

iter-177+: the body returns
`(CategoryTheory.toSheafify J (PicSharp.presheaf C))` (using Mathlib's
`Sites.ConcreteSheafification` API) — assuming `J` admits abelian-group
sheafification, which is provided by `HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat`
(supplied by Mathlib for the relevant universes via its
abelian-category-target sheafification). For the iter-176 file-skeleton
the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem unit {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
    Nonempty (PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicScheme C J).val) := by
  sorry

end PicScheme

end Scheme

end AlgebraicGeometry

233	/-! ## §3. The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent
234	
235	The structural lemma underlying Step 4 of Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main` is the
236	Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma**: given a surjection
237	`α : Z ⟶ P` of étale sheaves with `R := Z ×_P Z` representable, `Z`
238	quasi-projective, and the first projection `R ⟶ Z` smooth and proper,
239	the quotient `P` is representable by a quasi-projective scheme.
240	
241	Kleiman's proof:
242	1. `R ↪ Z ×_k Z` is a flat (from (iv)) and proper monomorphism, hence a
243	   closed immersion by EGA IV 8.11.5.
244	2. By the theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
245	   quasi-projective schemes (Altman–Kleiman), there exist a
246	   quasi-projective scheme `Q` and a faithfully flat, projective map
247	   `Z ⟶ Q` with `R = Z ×_Q Z`. The map `Z ⟶ Q` is smooth (its fibers
248	   coincide with those of `R ⟶ Z`, which are smooth).
249	3. In the category of étale sheaves, both `Q` and `P` are coequalisers of
250	   `R ⇒ Z`; uniqueness of the coequaliser gives `Q ≃ P`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_proper_quotient` (Kleiman §4 Lem.
253	`lm:qt`). -/
